text: Venice offers a refreshing green space amidst its bustling environment. While the area lacks grassy lawns and sufficient room for activities like running or kicking a ball, it provides a pleasant retreat. During my visit, the location was relatively calm, with some children playing and couples enjoying quiet moments together.
A hidden gem that many people rush past.  A great place to get out of the afternoon sun and sit down and cool off.  Clean and well maintained.  There is a playground for the kiddos.
Little park. Good place to have a rest in shadow before taking a bus.
Nice little place away from the main crowds, good spot to rest get some shaded seating and have a snack. Cool breeze from the canals nearby and close to main Venice entry points
Small and unsightly park. Good for kids because it’s one of the few places with a playground. Not worth a visit while you’re in Venice.

Lovely small park to catch a break from all the walking. Lots of trees and lots of shade in the park.

Lots of benches to sit and listen to the birds and the sound of water from the small little fenced fountain.

Clean park without trash. I did saw a dead pigeon floating in the fountain.

It's a nice, small park. There is a lot of comfy benchs so it's nice to sit around. But be careful after daylight especially if you have kids, because there is too many mosquitoes around the park. Maybe a anti-mosquitoe spray would be a good idea.
Beautiful Park with lots of seating and a playground.
The garden is very cozy and tranquil, but we went there specifically for the kids playground that, unfortunately, is temporarily closed

Had a wonderful time at the royal gardens. If you’re in San Marco Square, highly recommend taking a break from the hustle and bustle here. It’s free and if you’re lucky you’ll get a bench in the shade.
Miło odpocząć w upalne dni. Jest źródło wody

A very large park in the Castello area of Venice, far away from the crowds, and filled with walking paths, large trees, flowers and shrubs with many statues and monuments.

The park sits along the waterway giving you fantastic views of San Marcos square off in the distance and Lido, only a boat ride away.

Such an amazingly different area then the canals and buildings you see in most areas of Venice.

Worth a visit and maybe bring lunch to enjoy on one of the many benches.

The gardens were created in 1807 by order of Napoleon Bonaparte and designed by Gianantonio Selva as a public green space for the citizens of Venice. Today, they serve both as a peaceful retreat and as a major cultural venue. Since 1895, the gardens have hosted the Venice Biennale, beginning with its first International Art Exhibition. During the Biennale, approximately 65% of the gardens are dedicated to exhibition space, while the remaining 35% remains open as public parkland. Starting in 1907, individual nations began constructing their own pavilions within the gardens. Today, there are 29 national pavilions, making the gardens one of the central hubs for contemporary art during the Biennale.
Walking paths, trees, bushes, and grass. Pretty view of the lagoon with Venice skyline in distance. (And Public WC/bathroom for 1 euro.)
Good park

Nice, quiet respite along the Fundamente between Arsenale and Giardini. Nice park with exhibits of sculpture changing every year. Also nice benches to contemplate the artwork. Some sculpture is interactive.

Lovely garden a bit along the way from at Marco square.
It's much quieter and peaceful with lots of seating and statues (many rather old and worn though).
Lovely to walk round and take in the smells of the plants.
There is an exhibition within which you need to pay to enter (which we didn't)
There are toilets but they also have to be paid for to be used.
There is a lovely pond which an old statue but best of all has lots and lots of terrapins - when we went there were lots of young ones  in there too.
Vibe is nature, old and terrepin

peaceful, green, and right by the water. I stumbled upon it while walking from San Marco, and ended up staying longer than I expected. There were some modern sculptures on display which made it feel like a mini outdoor museum. Perfect place to rest, read, or just escape the crowds for a while

Very cool place, awesome art and arch installation! Highly recommended!
Thanks you for providing sublime art :)
One of the greenest places in Venice and close to the exhibition centre. We were lucky and we could see some architecture solution for free. Playgrounds for kids.

Lovely little green space to relax in with some interesting and creatively designed pieces to interact with. From what I can tell the featured interactive pieces update sometimes. I wish I took more pics.

Originally the site of shipyards, the gardens were transformed into public gardens in the 1930s. Located just a 10-minute walk from Piazza San Marco, the gardens offer a peaceful spot to rest and recharge away from the busier parts of the city. During the Venice Biennale, the gardens serve as an outdoor exhibition space for contemporary art and architecture, making it both a cultural venue and a quiet retreat for locals and visitors alike.

Went there to visit Bienale Arte di Venezia.
There is also a caffeteria where you can eat a delicious sandwich and a great cup of Italian coffee while visiting the pavillions.
Pics are from 2024.

Great place in Murano to have some fresh air and to relax under the trees. There are tables, so you can bring your own food and take a seat. Also there's a small playground so children can have fun too.
Dogs are allowed in the area.
Nice quiet park with trees, benches and grass. Play structure with 2 slides and swings. Good place to take a break, eat lunch and let kids play.
Credo sia una passeggiata che ogni anima innamorata di Venezia si meriti almeno una volta.

The bienalle for architecture is both mind blowing and overwhelming. The 2025 installation was very focused on climate change, global growth and usage of resources and how materials/technologies are used for global problem solving. It is important to go in with an open mind and time to process the information. Some countries and exhibits are better than others in terms of interpretation and information. For the year 2025, the beauty is in the details. The exhibition u Is not about architecture as many think of it, but rather about elements of architecture. And, if you are not comfortable with the English or Italian language, you will likely be lost and confused. It is wise to get the three-day pass and break up your time to allow the ability to process the density of information. There are many restaurants and cafes in the Giardini but in the main bienalle exhibit in the arsenale area there is only one large cafe and restaurant. Be sure to download area maps before you explore to get a good sense of place and understand the exhibits before you explore. The water taxis will bring you to the main stops for arsenale and giardini.

Compared to the rest of Venice, I found Giardini to be incredibly peaceful. Unlike much of the city, it's lush with greenery—trees, flowers, and open spaces. There are plenty of benches, and I enjoyed watching birds and butterflies flutter around. I came for the Biennale, but it was the tranquility of Giardini that truly impressed me.

Beautiful location for the biennial art and architecture Biennales. At the Giardini are most of the national pavilions. At the 2025 Architecture Biennale I was particularly impressed by the US and Scandinavian pavilions with the former focusing on the porch in US architecture with great examples like Ocean Grove, New Jersey and Chautauqua, New York. The Greek pavilion was impressive as well with a focus on the two Venetian shipyards on the island of Crete. The Israeli pavilion was closed for renovation as was the pavilion of Venezuela, designed by Carlo Scarpa (1906-1978) who also designed the original entrance pavilion to the Biennale.
Amazing garden with many sculptures. Wonderful view, great for a walk during a warm day. Fountains, playground and toilets available in the park.

A very quiet garden in Venice, this part doesn’t have many tourists like the other places in Venice and it’s quite a nice and chill distraction from the commotion happening near St Marco, highly recommend for anyone to pass there!
It’s a really nice place for picnic, there is a huge slope where everyone enjoy themselves like napping or just seeing the beautiful scene

Amid the bustling canals and throngs of visitors that define Venice, the Giardini della Biennale offers a breath of calm. This leafy enclave, tucked along the Bacino di San Marco, isn’t a destination for grand botanical wonders, but its quiet charm and historical roots make it a worthy detour for those seeking respite.

Originally marshland transformed by Napoleon into public gardens, the space now serves dual purposes: a peaceful retreat and a cultural hub. Its winding paths lead visitors past 30 pavilions, each representing a different nation and showcasing architectural flair from icons like Carlo Scarpa and Alvar Aalto. Though the structures truly come alive during the Venice Biennale Art Festival—when global creativity takes center stage—they retain a quiet magnetism year-round.

The gardens’ unpretentious beauty is amplified by quirky details: feral cats lounging in sunlit corners, the stoic gaze of Garibaldi’s statue at the entrance, and a serene coastal walk that skirts the water’s edge. These elements stitch together a patchwork of tranquility, a stark contrast to the nearby chaos of St. Mark’s Square.

Yet, for all its understated appeal, the Giardini della Biennale remains a “nice-to-have” rather than a must-see. If your Venice itinerary is packed, you won’t miss a life-changing experience by skipping it. But for those with moments to spare, it’s a gentle reminder that even in a city overrun by tourism, pockets of stillness—and a few curious cats—still endure.

Stopped off here just to see for ourselves what the gardens were like and not really impressed.  Lots of benches to sit and rest, but statues not really that prominent.
Did stop and grab a drink at the really nice Paradiso restaurant with a fabulous view into the bay area.
bel parchetto nascosto agli occhi dei più, oserei dire un piccolo locus amoenus
Frumoas colt de rai!

This was my second time in Venice, and visiting the Giardini Reali truly felt like a refreshing escape in the heart of the city.

I spent almost two hours here, simply enjoying the flowers, their scents, the peaceful walkways, and the calm beauty of it all.
It’s a quiet, green corner filled with life, color, and charm, a wonderful experience on its own.

Whether you're a nature lover or just need a break from the busy streets of Venice, this garden is an absolute gem worth visiting. ♥️

This is a gorgeous garden in Venice and super peaceful. It is nice to sit down and take a break or stroll around and see the different flowers and greenery. It is in a prime position right near the water and eateries nearby too.
Lovely gardens which offer some well needed shade. Sadly the park is often really busy, so it's sometimes rather difficult to find a place to sit down.
Beautiful gardens! It was so nice to see such beautiful green gardens since we’d been in stone cities for several days.

“Tiny green escape in the heart of Venice—surprisingly refreshing! 🌿”

Stumbled upon Giardini Reali just behind Piazza San Marco, and it instantly became my go-to oasis in the city. With shaded benches, pergola-covered walkways, and a lovely view of the lagoon, it’s the perfect spot to recharge between sightseeing bursts  .

Unexpected calm: Even on busy days, the garden’s tucked-away location makes it feel almost secret—perfect for a pause with a book or a snack  .

Lovely design touches: A restored 19th-century greenhouse, drawbridge to Piazza San Marco, and the longest wisteria pergola in Venice give it both charm and elegance  .

Free and easy access: Open to the public, and ideal for hanging out while you wait for the vaporetto at Giardinetti stop. No tickets, no rush  .

Tucked away just around the corner from the bustling St. Mark’s Square, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation (Giardini della Fondazione Venezia) are a true hidden gem—a quiet haven of greenery and tranquility in a city better known for its canals and narrow alleys. We stumbled upon this secret spot during our visit and ended up spending a wonderfully peaceful hour here, simply relaxing with an ice cream and enjoying the natural beauty.

The gardens have an interesting history, originally part of the private grounds of a noble Venetian palace. Today, they are one of the few green spaces in the historic center, offering a rare escape from the crowds. The well-maintained paths wind through a delightful mix of Mediterranean plants, shady trees, and blooming flowers, creating an atmosphere that feels worlds away from the nearby tourist hotspots.

One of the most enjoyable aspects was the sense of seclusion—even though we were only minutes from the heart of Venice, the gardens felt almost undiscovered. The benches and quiet corners make it an ideal spot to pause, unwind, and soak in the beauty of the surroundings.

For anyone looking for a moment of calm in the midst of Venice’s energy, this is the perfect place. Whether you’re escaping the midday heat, looking for a quiet place to read, or simply wanting to appreciate another side of the city, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation are well worth seeking out.

The Giardini Reali in Venice, also known as the Royal Gardens, offer a serene escape from the bustling streets of the city. These historic gardens, originally created for royalty, boast lush greenery, vibrant flowers, and tranquil pathways perfect for leisurely strolls. Visitors can admire the elegant statues and fountains scattered throughout the park, while enjoying panoramic views of the Venetian lagoon. Whether relaxing on a bench or exploring the hidden corners, the Giardini Reali provide a peaceful retreat and a glimpse into Venice's regal past.

I really liked this small cozy garden located near the center.  There is a lot of shade, many benches to rest after a walk, and beautiful flowers around (I was there in the middle of July).  Here you can hide from the scorching sun.  The road is paved with small stones.
I got humbled in a game of cards by pretty girl. The view is great too

Un luogo tranquillo, popolato di cicale che accompagnano allegre la tua permanenza qui. Di quando in quando nel canale passa una barca, l'acqua pigola sulla riva, sbatte e poi si scosta,  rientrando nei ranghi del flusso principale. Alberi, cespugli, arbusti, prato. Tutto è verde e rilassante.
The park is closed to the public. But the street is really quite and looks over one of the many canals

The park in Italy was simple but beautiful, with wide stretches of bright green grass that looked almost painted under the sunlight. It was the kind of place where everything felt calm and open. The grass was soft and freshly cut, carrying that clean, earthy smell that comes after a morning watering. People sat on blankets or benches, talking quietly or just watching the day go by.
Children ran across the open field, their shoes kicking up tiny bits of grass as they played, and every so often a light breeze would move through, making the leaves in the nearby trees rustle softly. There were a few old stone paths that curved through the park, leading to shaded spots where the grass grew a little taller and wilder.
From one end, you could see the red roofs of nearby houses and hear the faint sounds of a café — clinking cups, laughter, and the hiss of an espresso machine. It wasn’t a large park, but it had a peaceful feeling, like it belonged to the people who used it every day. The green grass stretched out like a soft carpet, inviting anyone to sit, rest, or just enjoy the view. It was simple, sunny, and perfectly Italian in its quiet charm

Nice, well- equipped playground close to the beach in a small park. Useful for families with small children.

Fino a qualche anno fa quando c'era il custode era tenuto benissimo. Curato e pulito. Ora è lasciato allo sbando. Nonostante questo, molto frequentato essendo ritrovo di genitori con bambini e cani con i loro padroni.

Perfect place to chill and read after a little walk!
Not crowded place, there are a few tourists.
An area for the intrepid who wants to experience Venice in less crowded quarters.

This square ended up being our favorite thing while visiting Venice. Don't ask why the kids loved it as much as they did, but it gave them something to do for hours while giving us a place to sit in shade and observe the locals.

The pigeon to seagull ratio was perfect, the water fountain gave the chance for the kids to cool themselves down, and there were more locals chilling at the square than tourists. It shouldn't have been as perfect of a place to spend a couple hours once or twice, but it just happened to be the perfect Venezian playground for our kids. Thanks pigeons and the occasional seagull. P.S, one of the locals told us that chasing the seagulls could be a bit dangerous for kids, and they may be right, but our kids did drive the fear of God into the heart of every flying apparatus on this square.

This square has shaded benches, a rarity in Venice. Nice enough place to sit and there is a water fountain to cool off too. We watched a seagull murder and devour a pigeon, which was something of a spectacle.
Don’t go too early nothing to see really
Mały placyk z ławkami i gołębiami

A very lovely peaceful park for Venetians, visitors, and children. They offer a play area for children and dogs (cani). My low score is the signs on the two dog play areas. The picture on the left clearly shows a large (grande) dog, and on the right a small (petit) dog. Yet both days there were very large dogs, over 40 lbs (18 kilos) in both areas. I asked in confusion why the very large dogs were in the small dog area and I was told it didn't matter, and yet the pictures told a different story. Why have any picture if they mean nothing. Maybe I'm reading too much into it. But if I am right, it would be a good idea to print a weight restriction on the sign, 12 kilos and under for small dogs.

Good beach to have a beach day. The water has good temperature and the place ia Clean as well. Keep an eye in the places with the sun umbrellas. If you lay down there will a Guys appear at some Point and want you to pay 10€ for using those.
Too much crowded. Kids can enjoy beache
リドに来たからには、やることもなくて海岸を散歩。とはいえ映画祭やってるホテルまでは1.4㎞もあるというし、そこら辺をブラブラ。日本の海岸と似ている感じだな。砂が入りそうになるのは怖いけど、白い部分には貝殻が堆積してて歩きやすい。10月だっていうのに、ビーチがあるせいか暑くて半袖で過ごしたくなってきた。流石に海に入る人はいないが日光浴している人はいる。

Milion ludzi. Gość wypożycza parasole plażowe za 10€ na cały dzień. Płytko. Jest gdzie kupić picie. Od portu można podjechać autobusem A. O jeździ w kółko po półwyspie. Woda w morzu na dzień 16.08.25 bardzo bardzo ciepła. Są przebieralnie, prysznic, WC - opłata 2€.

Пляж досить гарний, але людей дуже багато — що й не дивно, адже він безкоштовний. На місці можна взяти в оренду парасольку, є туалет, душова та перевдягальня, проте за кожне потрібно доплачувати окремо. Лежаків, на жаль, немає 😅 трохи було ніяково, коли ми знайшли місце щоб сісти на пісочку (ми не займали лежанок чи тощо)  а прийшов робітник з сусіднього пляжу і почав нас виганяти бо наче б то ми перетнули межу безкоштовного і їхнього, нам довелося переносити речі буквально на 2 метри 🤦🏻‍♀️

Why there is no one mentioned about how beautiful these trees inside the island? And is it not magical enough to stay in a land that above waterplace? Funny enough!

Come here, take some B&W pictures, visit San Michele all'Isola church and cloister then slow down. Just walk through the pebbled paths and feel the silence, and the inner peace. Pray on a random grave and then find the way out, to the sea, the purring boats and the screaming seagulls.
The Cemetery on the island of  San Michele  is a unique place that worth visiting.
Next to his wive Vera, Igor found a wonderful place to be buried. Also don't miss Diaghilev's grave alongside the same wall but on the other side of the doorway.

Thomb of Igor Fyodorovich Stravinsky[a] ComSE (17 June [O.S. 5 June] 1882 – 6 April 1971) was a Russian composer, pianist and conductor, later of French (from 1934) and American (from 1945) citizenship. He is widely considered one of the most important and influential composers of the 20th century and a pivotal figure in modernist music.
Grave, silence and beauty .

Surprising oasis of peace in a hectic city.  Come here, sit, walk your dog and enjoy the quiet.
Parco di Villa Groggia is a serene, lesser-known park in Venice's Cannaregio district, offering a peaceful retreat from the city's bustle. Visitors can enjoy shaded paths, benches, and the charming Teatrino Groggia, a small 19th-century theater hosting community events .

Historically, the park occupies the site of the 16th-century Palazzo Donà, demolished in 1823. In the late 19th century, Giuseppe Groggia developed the area into a romantic-style garden. After his death, the property was bequeathed to the San Gioacchino Institute in Mestre for the education of underprivileged girls. Since 1975, the City of Venice has owned and maintained the park .

Today, Parco di Villa Groggia serves as a tranquil space for locals and visitors alike, featuring shaded areas, benches, and a community center, making it an ideal spot to relax and experience a quieter side of Venice.
A small park. Peaceful, quiet. Many ppl walk their dogs here too.

Delizioso angolo in una zona poco nota della città,  questo parco con annesso teatrino ospita il  centro civico e una simpatica ludoteca. Ampio abbastanza da chiamarsi parco è però di dimensioni non enormi e quindi entrandovi si può vedere la forma quadrangolare. Silenzioso, ombreggiato in estate e riparato in inverno è davvero una piccola oasi di pace dove riposare durante una passeggiata veneziana fuori dal super centro.
C'è ne vorrebbero di più

Un bel parco a Venezia. Vale la pena visitarlo, anche per uscire per un attimo dal frastuono turistico della città. Tranquillo, con zone in ombra, un vero ristoro per la mente e per le membra. Pulito e molto curato, ci si può riposare sulle numerose panchine, bere e dissetarsi alle sue fontanelle con acqua potabile, distendersi e rilassarsi prima di riprendere il viaggio. Lo consiglio.

Originally the private garden of Palazzo Giustinian, this space was redesigned in 1689 by Marco Giustinian, Bishop of Torcello. The garden was restored in the 1990s and is now part of the Murano Glass Museum. It is open to visitors free of charge after museum admission, offering a quiet retreat within the historic grounds.
After spending an hour in the glass museum it was a literal breath of fresh air. I love old trees and to know these were planted here intentionally so long ago...
Lovely park for walking around with nice weather. It has water fountains every once in a while, so you can drink or fill your water bottle. The scale of it is quite impressive as well

Parco San Giuliano …. One of the most beautiful parks in Venice...a large park. Close to the lake. There is a bar, seating areas, children's games, toilets, and parking. It is a suitable place for families or for practicing sports in the open air
Perfect place to spend the time !

Beautiful area with lots of green space, a park in the heart of Mestre and just across from Venice. The perfect place for complete rest and relaxation. Inside the park are great restaurants and bars that offer good food and drink. The green area offers a good view of Venice and the Venetian lagoon. Very open, with lots of trails for both runners and walkers and cyclists. Everything is great done. It is easy to walk or run 4-5 km around the park, the lagoon and its trails. Apart from being an area for environmental and landscape recovery, it is also one of the most important places to study the lagoon environment, it is also popular as a destination for sports and cultural activities for leisure. An ideal place to stay in nature with the family. Created thanks to the European contribution, San Giuliano Park is an ambitious project, one of the most significant urban conversion and land reclamation interventions in Italy due to its complexity and articulation, as well as its dimensional and territorial characteristics. San Giuliano Park is one of the richest areas of biodiversity in the Venetian lagoon environment, and is known for being an excellent area for bird watching to visit the lagoon, attracting the interest of many nature photographers and observers, especially ornithologists. The park is actually known to enthusiasts for its rich avifauna, as it is a nesting area for many species of birds.
Want to go on a picnic? Want to go for a run, feel like jogging or even just to get fresh air? This is your place. No matter the season, you’ll love it.
Absolutely stunning! Clean, well organized. A lot of space for different kind of activities. Nice spot for walking, running and bicycling. Amazing view of Venice lagoon.

I heard that this is the biggest park in Europe. It can be true, it's very very big and suitable for jogging, running and having a picnic with many people. There are parks as well. The reason why I gave 3 stars is that it was so empty. No enough trees, as you see in the pictures I uploaded, green flat areas are a lot but trees are not very much. There are football pitches and basketball areas too. I would recommend here for taking a walk and having fresh air.

Super awesome park just next to the bridge to Venice. Great for long walks, absolutely great for inline skating on a butterly smooth track and around the park, cycle paths, basketball court, place for landkiting, outdoor fitness and more. It is 3m away from camper stop :) simply the best park around here.

This was a beautiful experience. There is a small tour which gives you enough information to inform and make the experience meaningful, but the tour guide allows you to experience the walk and music with yourself. The music composed specifically for the walk through the labyrinth is gorgeous, and walking quietly through while listening is a meditative experience, even if you are not a Borges readers. It was lightly raining, with sun, while we were there which added to the beauty!

We did this as a paired tour with the Vatican Chapels, and together they were an excellent experience.

We would love to experience it in another season, and will probably go back! Thank you!

Know before you go:
-You must book a ticket in advance through the Cini Foundation website.
-This is a symbolic labyrinth, not meant to be like a tourist corn maze or the like, more akin to a meditation garden.
-You should bring your own headphones if you can.

The maze is 1km to walk thru. There is a wonderful story behind the maze that the tour explains.
Booking the tour is the only way to walk through the maze also
Richtig schöne Promenade.. Zeitraum ende Juli / anfang  august.. In der Richtung  ging jeden Tag die Sonne auch unter. Man ist dort ganz am Rand.  Richtig schön

Вдоль набережной есть лавочки,можно отдохнуть,всё чисто …много чаек,мы кормили их,но оказывается,что нельзя…из-за того что они …. на тротуаре…🤷‍♀️

Ein wirklich entspannender Ort inmitten der quirligen Stadt Venedig. Hier ist ein botanischer Garten nach dem Buch der Heiligen Theresa angelegt worden. Das Consorzio Vini Venezia kultiviert in diesem außergewöhnlichen Garten, 18 verschiedene Rebsorten aus denen auch Wein produziert wird. Verantwortlich ist hier Pater Hermano.
Die Mönche stellen auch verschiedene Heilmittel aus Melissa her, die in der Klosterapotheke käuflich erworben werden können!

Piccola oasi di verde che si trova tra la fermata del vaporetto ed il ponte per Burano.
Al suo interno, ci sono i resti della chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo ( ne rimane 6na cappella e l'alto campanile...).

Il parco di Mazzorbo si trova a poche decine di metri dalla fermata "Mazzorbo" del Vaporetto. Il parco, non grandissimo, è però ben tenuto, ben ventilato per la sua posizione a nordovest dell'isola. È ideale per riposare e stare in tranquillità, soprattutto quando la calca di Burano diventa insostenibile. Un muricciolo divide il parco dai vigneti di Venissa.
Parc au calme, très peu fréquenté, idéal pour se reposer.

Il parco Emmer, come avrebbero voluto da sempre abitanti e frequentatori, sta lentamente diventando una delle aree verdi più apprezzate del quartiere. Il parco si trova in Via P.Cornaglia a poca distanza dal Centro Commerciale Panorama. Il bellissimo parco offre un posto meraviglioso per rilassarsi, fare jogging, fare una piacevole passeggiata, divertirsi con i bambini o giocare a calcetto. Il parco prende il nome dal famoso architetto Pietro Emilio Emmer che costruì gran parte di Marghera nel 1919. Ha prestato particolare attenzione alle aree verdi e ai parchi.
Splendido parco, ben tenuto, molti alberi, ombra, e panchine.
